items.title: Match penalty to modeling goal
items.avoid: Defaulting to L1 whenever feature count is high.
items.prefer: Use L2 for shrinkage and correlated-feature stability; L1 when sparse interpretability is required.
items.why: L1 and L2 encode different inductive biases—sparsity vs smooth shrinkage.
items.example: For collinear economic indicators in a forecast model, Ridge (L2) often generalizes better than Lasso picking one arbitrary indicator.

items.title: Tune lambda on validation
items.avoid: Selecting the smallest λ that minimizes training loss.
items.prefer: Search a log-spaced grid of λ with k-fold CV; pick the minimum or one-standard-error rule.
items.why: Only held-out error reflects the bias–variance tradeoff the penalty is meant to optimize.
items.example: Plot CV error vs log(λ); choose λ at the valley, not at the left edge where training loss still falls.

items.title: Scale before penalizing linear weights
items.avoid: Fitting L1/L2 logistic or linear models on raw mixed-scale columns.
items.prefer: Pipeline: StandardScaler (fit on train) → penalized estimator.
items.why: Fair penalties require comparable coefficient scales across features.
items.example: make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), LogisticRegression(penalty='l2', C=1.0)) treats age and income equitably.

items.title: Read train vs validation before adjusting λ
items.avoid: Increasing λ whenever validation error is disappointing.
items.prefer: Increase λ only when train ≪ val (overfitting); decrease λ when train and val are both high.
items.why: Regularization fixes high-variance fits, not high-bias underfitting.
items.example: Train acc 98%, val acc 62% → try larger λ; train acc 58%, val acc 57% → try smaller λ or more capacity.
